mainEntity.name: How many square feet is a sheet of plywood?

acceptedAnswer.text: A standard 4×8 foot sheet of plywood covers 32 square feet. Other common sizes: 4×4 (16 sq ft), 4×10 (40 sq ft), and 4×12 (48 sq ft). To find sheets needed, divide your total area by 32 and add waste factor.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate plywood needed?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sheets = (Length × Width) ÷ 32 × (1 + waste%). For a 12×20 ft floor: 240 ÷ 32 = 7.5, with 10% waste: 7.5 × 1.10 = 8.25, round up to 9 sheets. For walls, calculate each wall's area, add them up, subtract openings, and divide by 32.

mainEntity.name: What thickness of plywood do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common thicknesses and uses: 1/4" for underlayment and backing, 3/8" for wall sheathing (non-structural), 1/2" for wall sheathing and cabinets, 5/8" for roof sheathing, and 3/4" for subfloor and structural applications. Subfloor plywood on 16" OC joists typically requires 3/4" (23/32") tongue-and-groove.

mainEntity.name: How much does a sheet of plywood cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prices vary by thickness, grade, and type. Approximate 4×8 sheet prices: 1/4" CDX $15-25, 1/2" CDX $25-40, 3/4" CDX $35-55, 3/4" sanded BC $50-70, 3/4" birch plywood $55-85, and 3/4" marine grade $80-120. Prices fluctuate with lumber markets.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between CDX and sanded plywood?

acceptedAnswer.text: CDX is construction grade with C-grade front, D-grade back, and X for exterior glue. It is rough, with knots and patches, used for sheathing, subfloor, and roofing. Sanded (BC, AB) plywood has smooth faces suitable for cabinets, furniture, and visible surfaces. Both are available in the same thicknesses.

mainEntity.name: How much waste should I plan for?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan for 10% waste on simple rectangular projects (floors, roofs). Add 15% for projects with angles and cutouts (walls with windows). Use 20% for complex shapes. If using plywood for irregular shapes or requiring grain matching, plan for 20-25% waste.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Plywood Sheets

Plywood calculations are straightforward: divide the total area by the sheet size, then add a waste factor for cuts and fitting.

Sheets = (Area ÷ 32) × (1 + Waste %)
  • Total area = length × width (in feet)
  • A standard 4×8 sheet covers 32 square feet
  • Divide area by 32 to get the minimum number of sheets
  • Add 10% waste for simple layouts, 15-20% for complex
  • Always round up to the next whole sheet
  • For multiple rooms or walls, add all areas together first
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many square feet is a sheet of plywood?

A standard 4×8 sheet covers 32 square feet. Also available: 4×10 (40 sq ft) and 4×12 (48 sq ft). Divide your total area by the sheet size to find how many sheets you need.

How do I calculate plywood needed?

Sheets = (Length × Width) ÷ 32 × (1 + waste%). For a 12×20 ft floor: 240 ÷ 32 = 7.5, with 10% waste = 8.25, round up to 9 sheets.

What thickness do I need?

1/4" for underlayment, 3/8" for non-structural sheathing, 1/2" for wall sheathing, 5/8" for roof sheathing, 3/4" for subfloor and structural. Check code for your specific application.

How much does plywood cost?

4×8 CDX sheet prices: 1/4" $15-25, 1/2" $25-40, 3/4" $35-55. Sanded/cabinet grade costs 30-50% more. Prices fluctuate with lumber markets.

CDX vs sanded plywood?

CDX has rough faces (C and D grade) with exterior glue — used for sheathing, subfloor, and roofing. Sanded plywood (BC, AB grades) has smooth faces for cabinets, furniture, and visible surfaces.

How much waste to plan for?

10% for rectangular areas, 15% for walls with windows/doors, 20% for complex shapes or grain-matched projects.
